stem: When is the frequency-encoding gradient applied?

options.text: Only during the initial 90-degree RF pulse
Between repetitions after all data have been stored
Only while the phase-encoding gradient is at zero
While the echo signal is being sampled

explanation: The readout gradient is active during signal acquisition, causing position along its axis to map to frequency. The sampled mixture of frequencies is later separated by Fourier transformation.

stem: How are positions distinguished along the frequency-encoding direction?

options.text: Spins at different positions precess at different frequencies during readout
Spins at different positions have permanently different T1 values
Each position receives a separate 180-degree RF pulse
Positions are distinguished only by receiver-coil size

explanation: The readout gradient adds a position-dependent magnetic field and therefore a position-dependent Larmor frequency. Frequency analysis maps those signal components back to spatial locations.

stem: What primarily determines the number of reconstructed pixels along the frequency-encoding axis?

options.text: The number of phase-encoding repetitions
The number of signal samples collected during readout
The number of slice-selection RF pulses
The number of receiver coils alone

explanation: Each sampled time point contributes to the frequency-domain data used for the readout matrix. Phase-encoding steps determine resolution along the orthogonal phase axis.

stem: What happens to dwell time when receiver bandwidth per sampled point is increased?

options.text: Dwell time becomes longer
Dwell time remains fixed by T1
Dwell time becomes shorter
Dwell time becomes equal to TR

explanation: Dwell time is the interval between successive samples and is inversely related to sampling bandwidth. Faster sampling supports a wider receiver bandwidth.

stem: In a conventional axial image, which gradient axis is commonly assigned to frequency encoding?

options.text: The z axis
The longitudinal magnetization axis only
The axis must always be identical to phase encoding
The x axis

explanation: With z used for axial slice selection, x is often selected as the readout direction and y as phase encoding. Frequency and phase assignments can be swapped for artifact control.
